required.title: [Close Reading Texts] A selection of rich, complex passages across genres
[Fiction] At least two novels read closely
required.author: Natalie Babbitt, *The Giver* by Lois Lowry, *Island of the Blue Dolphins* by Scott O'Dell, *The Witch of Blackbird Pond* by Elizabeth George Speare, *Holes* by Louis Sachar, or a comparable text. These are read slowly, with annotation and discussion

required.title: [Nonfiction] At least one nonfiction book or collection
required.author: David Macaulay, *Lincoln: A Photobiography* by Russell Freedman, *The Story of Science* by Joy Hakim, or chapters from age-appropriate science and history texts. Nonfiction requires different close reading skills from fiction.

required.title: [Script Instruction] Materials for two additional scripts

suggested.title: [Poetry] A collection of poetry for close reading
suggested.author: Michael Driscoll, or selected poems by Robert Frost, Emily Dickinson, Langston Hughes, and Shel Silverstein. Poetry is the genre that most rewards close reading.

suggested.title: [History of Writing] Empires of the Word
suggested.author: Nicholas Ostler

suggested.title: [Annotation Supply] Pencils and a good eraser, or colored pens and sticky notes

competence_markers.description: The student can perform a close reading of a passage using the three-read method and produce substantive annotations
The student uses comprehension strategies (predicting, questioning, clarifying, summarizing, visualizing, connecting) as habitual practices during reading
The student can identify the organizational structure of a text and explain how the structure serves the author's purpose
The student makes supported inferences — reading between the lines with textual evidence rather than unsupported guessing
The student can decode text in at least two scripts beyond Latin (sounding out words even without language comprehension)
The student can narrate the history of writing — its invention, the development of the Phoenician alphabet, and the diversification of modern scripts
The student continues to read widely and independently, now with greater depth and attention
Written narrations and responses show growing sophistication — detail, structure, and analytical thinking

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through your annotation of this passage.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you notice? What did you underline and why? What questions did you write in the margins?

narration_prompts.prompt: Summarize this chapter in your own words.
narration_prompts.detail: Then tell me one thing the author implied but did not state directly.
Why do you think the author chose this structure? Would a different structure have worked better?

narration_prompts.prompt: What did you predict would happen at this point in the book?
narration_prompts.detail: Were you right? What clues did the author give — or withhold?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of how writing was invented.
narration_prompts.detail: Why was it invented? What problem did it solve? How did the Phoenician alphabet change things?
Sound out the words for me. What can you figure out about the meaning even without knowing the language?

cross_connections.description: The history of writing is directly part of civilizational history. Cuneiform, hieroglyphics, and the Phoenician alphabet are pivotal developments in the story of civilization that 1.1.1 tells. Close reading skills are the tools the student uses to engage with primary historical sources.

Close reading and good writing are deeply connected. The student who reads closely — noticing how authors structure sentences, choose words, and organize ideas — absorbs the craft of writing. Reading like a writer and writing like a reader reinforce each other.

The structural analysis of language in 3.1.1 connects to text structure analysis in 3.1.2. The student who understands sentence structure can parse complex sentences during close reading. The student who understands text structure can navigate complex arguments.

Close reading of religious, philosophical, and ideological texts requires the skills developed here. The student who can identify an author's assumptions, trace an argument's structure, and read between the lines is equipped for the comparative worldview analysis of 1.3.1.
Script literacy connects directly to phonological knowledge. The student who understands how Arabic or Devanagari encodes sounds is integrating script literacy with phonological awareness.

Close reading is not just for literary texts. The ability to read a mathematics problem closely — identifying what is given, what is asked, and what is implied — uses the same comprehension strategies developed here.

content.why_this_matters:
You can read fluently. Words on a page no longer require effort to decode — your brain does that automatically now, and your attention is free to focus on meaning. This is an enormous achievement, and it opens everything that follows.

But there is a difference between reading and reading well. A person can read fluently and still miss what a text actually says. They can finish a chapter and not remember what happened. They can read an argument and not notice that it is flawed. They can read a passage full of clues and miss every one of them. Fluent reading is necessary. It is not sufficient.

This packet teaches you how to read closely — to slow down, pay attention, and engage with a text as an active participant rather than a passive receiver. Close reading means treating the text as something worth your full attention. It means noticing not just what the text says but how it says it, what it assumes, what it implies, and what it leaves out. It means annotating — marking the text with your own thoughts as you read, so that reading becomes a conversation between you and the author.

These skills are not just for literature class. The person who can read closely reads everything better — science textbooks, historical documents, legal contracts, news articles, instruction manuals, and the arguments of people trying to persuade them. Close reading is a defense against being misled, a tool for deeper understanding, and the foundation of every kind of analytical work you will do from this point forward.

This packet also advances your script literacy. You will learn to decode text in at least two scripts beyond Latin and begin to understand the structural logic behind the world's major writing systems. The history of writing — how this extraordinary technology was invented, spread, and diversified — becomes part of your knowledge.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Close reading skills** — the ability to read a text attentively, noticing structure, word choice, implications, and what is left unsaid
2. **Annotation as a thinking practice** — the habit of marking text with questions, observations, and connections while reading
3. **Comprehension strategies** — specific, teachable strategies for making meaning: predicting, questioning, clarifying, summarizing, visualizing, and connecting
4. **Text structure awareness** — understanding how different types of text are organized and using that structure to aid comprehension
5. **Inference** — reading between the lines to understand what a text implies but does not state directly
6. **Functional decoding in two additional scripts** — the ability to sound out words in at least two scripts beyond Latin
7. **The history of writing** — understanding how writing was invented, how scripts developed and spread, and what this reveals about civilization
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Close Reading Is — And Why It Matters

Close reading means reading a text more than once, each time with a different lens. The first read is for understanding — what is the text about? The second read is for structure — how is the text organized? What choices did the author make? The third read is for depth — what does the text imply? What is left unsaid? What assumptions does it make? How does it connect to other things you know?

Most people read everything once. They get the gist — the general idea — and move on. This is fine for most everyday reading. But for texts that matter — texts that will shape your thinking, inform your decisions, or test your ability to understand complex ideas — a single reading is not enough.

**The close reading process:**

1. **First read: What does it say?** Read the whole passage through. Do not stop. Get the overall picture. What is this about? What happened? What is the main idea?

2. **Second read: How does it say it?** Read again, slower. Notice the structure. Where does the text shift from one idea to another? What words stand out? What repetitions appear? How does the author organize the information or the story? Mark the text — underline key sentences, circle important words, write questions in the margins.

3. **Third read: What does it mean?** Read the marked portions again. Why did the author make these choices? What is implied but not stated? What does the author assume the reader already knows? What is the author trying to make you think or feel? How does this connect to other things you have read?

This three-read process is not a formula to follow mechanically. It is a stance toward text — a habit of attention that transforms reading from passive reception into active thinking.

### Part 2: Annotation — Making Your Thinking Visible

Annotation is the practice of writing on and around a text as you read it. It turns reading into a conversation between you and the author — you respond, question, agree, argue, and connect as you go.

**What to annotate:**

- **Underline or highlight** sentences that seem important, surprising, or confusing.
- **Circle words** you do not know or that seem deliberately chosen.
- **Write questions in the margins:** "Why does the author say this?" "What does this mean?" "Is this true?"
- **Note connections:** "This is like what we read about Mesopotamia" or "This contradicts what the other book said."
- **Mark structure:** Note where the text shifts from one idea to another. Label sections: introduction, evidence, counterargument, conclusion.
- **Record reactions:** "I disagree" or "This surprises me" or "I need to think about this more."

Annotation is not about making the text look marked up. It is about making your thinking visible — so you can return to the text later and see not just what the author said but what you thought about it.

**A practical note:** Annotate with pencil in books you own. Use sticky notes in library books or shared texts. Keep a reading journal for extended responses that do not fit in the margins. The format matters less than the habit.

### Part 3: Comprehension Strategies — Tools for Making Meaning

Research on reading comprehension has identified specific strategies that strong readers use, often without knowing it. Weaker readers can be taught these strategies explicitly, and doing so significantly improves their comprehension.

**Predicting:** Before reading and during reading, the strong reader anticipates what will come next. "Based on what has happened so far, I think the character will..." or "The title suggests this article will be about..." Prediction keeps the reader actively engaged and creates a framework for understanding.

**Questioning:** The strong reader generates questions while reading. "Why is the author telling me this?" "What does this word mean in this context?" "How does this connect to what I read earlier?" "Is this claim supported?" Questions drive attention and deepen processing.

**Clarifying:** When something is confusing, the strong reader stops and tries to figure it out. They reread. They look at the context. They check whether the confusion comes from an unfamiliar word, a complex sentence structure, or an idea they do not yet understand. Weak readers skip past confusion and lose the thread.

**Summarizing:** The strong reader periodically pauses to consolidate what they have read — "So far, the main idea is..." or "What has happened so far is..." Summarizing during reading (not just after) keeps the reader oriented in longer texts.

**Visualizing:** The strong reader creates mental images while reading — seeing the scene, imagining the diagram, picturing the process. Visualization is especially important for narrative text and for spatial or scientific information.

**Connecting:** The strong reader connects what they are reading to other texts, to their own experience, and to their existing knowledge. "This is like..." or "This reminds me of..." or "This connects to what we studied in 1.1.1 about the Indus Valley." Connections integrate new information into existing knowledge structures.

These strategies are not used in a fixed sequence. Strong readers deploy them flexibly — predicting when the text invites prediction, questioning when something seems wrong, clarifying when something is confusing. The goal is to internalize these strategies so deeply that they become automatic, the way decoding became automatic in Stretching Branches.

### Part 4: Text Structure — How Texts Are Built

Understanding how a text is organized helps you read it more effectively. Different structures serve different purposes, and recognizing the structure tells you where to direct your attention.

**Narrative structure:** Stories follow a pattern — characters are introduced in a setting, a problem or conflict arises, events unfold as characters respond to the conflict, and the story reaches a resolution. Recognizing this pattern helps you track the story's arc and anticipate its direction.

**Chronological structure:** Many nonfiction texts — history, biography, process descriptions — organize information in time order. First this happened, then this, then this. Recognizing chronological structure helps you track the sequence and understand cause and effect.

**Compare/contrast structure:** Some texts are organized around similarities and differences between two or more things. Recognizing this structure helps you identify what the author is comparing and why.

**Cause/effect structure:** Some texts are organized around why things happen. Recognizing this structure helps you identify the causal relationships the author is establishing — which events or conditions lead to which outcomes.

**Problem/solution structure:** Some texts present a problem and then discuss potential solutions. Recognizing this structure helps you evaluate whether the proposed solutions actually address the problem.

**Argument structure:** Some texts are organized as an argument — a claim supported by evidence and reasoning. Recognizing this structure is critical for analytical reading in B2 and beyond. You identify the claim, examine the evidence, and evaluate whether the reasoning is sound.

When you begin reading a text, ask: "How is this organized?" The answer shapes how you read. A narrative invites you to follow the story. A cause/effect text invites you to track the chains of consequence. An argument invites you to evaluate the reasoning. The structure is a map, and recognizing it helps you navigate.

### Part 5: Inference — Reading Between the Lines

Not everything a text communicates is stated directly. Authors imply, suggest, hint, and assume. A reader who can only understand what is stated explicitly — what is literally on the page — misses half of what a text conveys.

**Inference** is the act of combining what the text says with what you already know to understand what the text means.

Example: "Maria put on her coat, grabbed her umbrella, and looked out the window with a sigh." The text does not say it is raining. The text does not say Maria is unhappy about the weather. But from the coat, the umbrella, and the sigh, you infer both. That is inference — and it is the foundation of reading comprehension beyond the literal level.

**Practicing inference:**

- After reading a passage, ask: "What does this text imply that it does not state directly?"
- Look for emotional language: if a character "muttered" rather than "said," what does that tell you?
- Notice what the author omits: what questions does the text raise but not answer?
- Consider the author's perspective: what does the author seem to believe or value, even if they do not say so directly?

Inference is not guessing. It is reasoning from evidence — the same skill used in science, law, and historical analysis. A good inference is supported by specific details in the text. A bad inference ignores or contradicts the evidence. Learning to tell the difference is one of the most important analytical skills you will develop.

### Part 6: The History of Writing

You have been learning to read and to read well. Now step back and consider the technology itself: writing. Where did it come from? How did it spread? Why do different civilizations write so differently?

**Writing was invented, not discovered.** For tens of thousands of years, humans communicated only through speech. Speech is powerful but limited — it disappears the moment it is spoken, and it cannot travel farther than a voice can carry. Writing solved both problems: it made language permanent and portable. A message written on clay, papyrus, or paper can survive centuries and travel across continents.

**The earliest writing emerged around 3400 BCE in Mesopotamia.** Sumerian cuneiform — wedge-shaped marks pressed into wet clay — was invented for accounting: recording how much grain was stored, who owed what to whom. Writing was not invented for poetry or philosophy. It was invented for bookkeeping. But once it existed, people used it for everything.

**Writing was independently invented at least three times.** Sumerian cuneiform, Chinese writing, and Mesoamerican writing systems (used by the Maya and others) each developed independently, with no contact between them. This tells us something important: writing is a solution that human beings reach for when societies become complex enough to need permanent records.

**The Phoenician alphabet changed everything.** Around 1050 BCE, the Phoenicians — traders who operated across the Mediterranean — developed a simple alphabet of about 22 consonant letters. This was far simpler than cuneiform or hieroglyphics, which required hundreds or thousands of symbols. The Phoenician alphabet was so efficient that it became the ancestor of almost every alphabet in use today: Greek, Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, and Hebrew all descend from the Phoenician original.

**Major script families today:**

- **Latin alphabet** — descended from Phoenician through Greek and Etruscan. Used for English, Spanish, French, German, Indonesian, Turkish, Vietnamese, and hundreds of other languages. The most widely used script in the world.
- **Cyrillic alphabet** — developed in the 9th century CE by followers of the Byzantine missionaries Cyril and Methodius. Based on Greek. Used for Russian, Ukrainian, Bulgarian, Serbian, Mongolian, and Central Asian languages.
- **Arabic script** — descended from Phoenician through Nabataean. An abjad (consonantal alphabet). Used for Arabic, Farsi, Urdu, Pashto, and historically for Turkish, Malay, and others. Written right to left.
- **Devanagari** — a Brahmic script used for Hindi, Sanskrit, Marathi, and Nepali. An abugida (each consonant symbol inherently carries a vowel sound, which is modified by diacritical marks). Organized by phonetic principles.
- **Chinese characters** — independently invented. Logographic (each character represents a word or morpheme, not a sound). Used for Chinese, and borrowed historically by Japanese, Korean, and Vietnamese. The largest character set of any writing system — tens of thousands of characters, though literacy requires approximately 3,000–4,000.
- **Korean Hangul** — created in 1443 by scholars working under King Sejong. The most deliberately designed writing system in widespread use. Letters represent sounds, and their shapes reflect the position of the tongue and mouth during articulation. Syllable blocks group letters into tidy visual units.

This is not just history. It is the map of human literacy across the world. The script a person reads determines which texts they can access, which signs they can navigate, and which cultures they can engage with directly. The student who reads only Latin script is literate in one system. The student who can decode several scripts has a wider world.

### Part 7: Functional Decoding in Two Additional Scripts

Building on your A2 introduction to a second script, you will now develop functional decoding in two scripts beyond Latin. "Functional decoding" means you can look at text in the script and sound out the words — even if you do not know the language. This is script literacy as distinct from language competence: you can decode without comprehending, and decoding is the first step toward comprehension.

Your mentor will guide you through the two scripts most relevant to your context and goals. For each script, you will:

1. Learn the basic characters and the sounds they represent
2. Understand the script's structural logic — how it organizes sounds into visual symbols
3. Practice reading simple words and short texts
4. Develop the ability to pick up a printed text in the script and begin sounding it out

This is a practical skill with immediate applications. The student who can decode Cyrillic can read signs in Moscow. The student who can decode Arabic script can sound out words across the Middle East and South Asia. The student who knows the principles of Chinese characters can begin extracting meaning from characters they have never seen before. Script literacy is the physical infrastructure of global literacy.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Close Reading Practice [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
At least twice per week, the student works through a close reading of a selected passage using the three-read method. First read for understanding, second read with annotation, third read for depth and discussion. The mentor discusses the annotated passage with the student, focusing on what they noticed, what questions they raised, and what inferences they made. Passages should vary in genre — fiction, nonfiction, poetry, historical documents.

### Study 2: Novel Study [Two Novels During the Packet]
The student reads two novels closely — not speed-reading for plot but reading attentively with annotation. After each chapter, the student writes a brief narration or response. After completing each novel, the student and mentor discuss: What was the author doing? How was the story structured? What did the author imply but not state? What themes emerged? What choices did the author make about language and structure?

### Study 3: Comprehension Strategy Practice [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Explicit instruction and practice with each comprehension strategy: predicting, questioning, clarifying, summarizing, visualizing, connecting. The student practices each strategy with a specific text, then begins combining strategies naturally during independent reading. The goal is internalization — the strategies should become habits, not checklists.

### Study 4: Text Structure Analysis [Mid-Packet]
The student reads texts organized in different structures — narrative, chronological, compare/contrast, cause/effect, problem/solution, argument — and identifies the structure of each. For at least three texts, the student creates a visual map of the structure: a story arc for narrative, a timeline for chronological, a Venn diagram for compare/contrast, a flowchart for cause/effect.

### Study 5: The History of Writing [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Dedicated study of how writing was invented and how scripts developed and spread. The student traces the path from Sumerian cuneiform through the Phoenician alphabet to the Latin, Cyrillic, and Arabic scripts. Separately, the student studies the independent invention of Chinese writing and Mesoamerican writing systems. The student creates a script family tree showing the relationships.

### Study 6: Second and Third Script Acquisition [Across the Packet]
Systematic instruction in two scripts beyond Latin. The student spends approximately 15 minutes per session, three to four times per week, learning and practicing characters. By the end of the packet, the student can decode simple words in both scripts. The student practices with authentic text samples — menus, signs, headlines, children's book pages — from languages that use these scripts.

### Study 7: Inference Practice [Late Packet]
The student reads passages deliberately chosen because they require inference — texts where the meaning is not fully stated on the surface. After reading, the student identifies what they inferred, what textual evidence supports the inference, and what additional information they used (background knowledge, context, genre expectations). This practice bridges to the analytical reading of B2.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Annotation Quality
**Developing well:** The student's annotations show genuine thinking — questions that probe the text, connections to other knowledge, observations about language and structure. Annotations are specific, not generic. The student annotates because it helps them think, not because it was assigned.

**Developing slowly:** Annotations are sparse, generic ("interesting"), or absent. The student highlights randomly without purpose. Annotations are completed as a task rather than used as a thinking tool.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled your own annotation process? Have you discussed specific annotations with the student, showing them what makes an annotation useful? Does the student understand the purpose of annotation (making thinking visible) or do they see it as a chore?

### 2. Inference Ability
**Developing well:** The student can identify what a text implies and support their inference with specific textual evidence. They distinguish between what the text says and what it means. They notice subtext — what characters feel but do not say, what an author values but does not state.

**Developing slowly:** The student can only identify explicitly stated information. When asked "What does this passage imply?" they either repeat what it says or guess without evidence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Practice with low-stakes, high-interest texts. "The door slammed. Footsteps pounded up the stairs. A moment later, the music started — loud." Ask: "How is this person feeling? How do you know?" Start with obvious inferences and build to subtler ones.

### 3. Text Structure Recognition
**Developing well:** The student can identify how a text is organized and explain why the author chose that structure. They can create visual maps of text structure. They use structural awareness to navigate complex texts.

**Developing slowly:** The student does not notice how texts are organized. All nonfiction feels the same to them. They cannot explain why information is presented in a particular order.

**Diagnostic questions:** Use paired texts — two articles on the same topic organized differently. Ask: "How are these organized differently? Which is more effective for this topic? Why?"

### 4. Script Decoding Progress
**Developing well:** The student can look at text in two non-Latin scripts and sound out words. They understand the structural logic of each script (alphabet vs. abjad vs. syllabary vs. logographic). They show curiosity about unfamiliar scripts.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot recall characters between sessions. They see script learning as disconnected from their reading development.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the script work connected to something real? Can the student decode a Russian sign, an Arabic restaurant name, a Korean label? Practical application motivates retention.

### 5. Discussion Quality
**Developing well:** In close reading discussions, the student offers specific observations, asks genuine questions, responds to the mentor's points with their own thinking, and sometimes changes their mind based on new evidence. The discussion feels like a real intellectual exchange.

**Developing slowly:** The student waits for the mentor to provide interpretations. They agree with whatever the mentor says. Their contributions are brief and unelaborated.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you leaving enough silence? After asking a question, wait. Do not fill the pause. The student needs processing time. Are your questions genuinely open, or do they have a "right answer" that the student is trying to guess?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Over-Analyzing Every Text
Not every reading session needs to be a close reading exercise. The student should continue reading widely for pleasure — novels, graphic novels, nonfiction, magazines — without annotation or discussion. Close reading is a practice applied to selected texts, not a way of reading everything. If every book becomes a close reading assignment, the student will learn to dread books.

### Teaching Strategies as Ends in Themselves
"Today we are going to practice predicting." This is a necessary phase of instruction but should not last long. The strategies are tools for understanding text, not activities to perform. Once taught, they should dissolve into the student's natural reading practice. If the student is still naming strategies weeks later ("I am using my clarifying strategy"), the strategies have not been internalized.

### Selecting Texts That Are Too Difficult
Close reading requires texts that are complex enough to reward re-reading but accessible enough that the student can read them without struggling with basic comprehension. A text that the student cannot understand on a first read is too difficult for close reading — they need to comprehend before they can analyze.

### Neglecting Nonfiction
Many close reading programs focus almost entirely on literary fiction and poetry. This is a mistake. The ability to read nonfiction closely — to identify the structure of an argument, to notice what evidence is presented and what is omitted, to evaluate the author's reasoning — is at least as important as the ability to analyze a novel. Ensure that at least a third of close reading practice involves nonfiction.
